claims.flip_evidence_text: provided, however, that, solely with respect to the foregoing clause (A), in no event shall any change... (iv) any change occurring after the date hereof in accounting requirements or principles required by GAAP (or any authoritative interpretations thereof)... provided, however, that the exceptions set forth in clauses (i), (ii), (iii), (iv), (v) and (vii) shall only apply to the extent that such event... does not have a materially disproportionate impact on the Company and the Company Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, compared to other companies that operate in the industry and geographic markets in which the Company and the Company Subsidiaries operate.
claims.notes: Claim captures the core GAAP exception but omits material limitations: the exclusion applies solely to clause (A), only to changes occurring after the date of the agreement, and is subject to the disproportionate-impact carve-out relative to industry peers.

prompt:
You are a proxy legal annotator for a research pilot. Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list.

For each claim return:
- correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true
- load_bearing_label: yes or no
- flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string
- notes: optional short note

Contract excerpt:
“Material Adverse Effect” means any change, effect, event, occurrence, development, condition or fact that, individually or in the aggregate with all other changes, effects, events, occurrences, developments, conditions or facts, has had or would reasonably be expected to have a material adverse effect on (A) the business, condition (financial or otherwise), assets, liabilities or results of operations of the Company and the Company Subsidiaries, taken as a whole or (B) the ability of the Company to consummate the Transactions or perform its obligations hereunder; provided, however, that, solely with respect to the foregoing clause (A), in no event shall any change, effect, event, occurrence, development, condition or fact resulting from or relating to any of the following, alone or in combination, be deemed to constitute, nor be taken into account in determining whether there has been, or there is reasonably expected to be, a Material Adverse Effect: (i) any change in general political, social, geopolitical or regulatory conditions, (ii) any change in economic, financial, commodity, credit or capital market conditions, including interest, foreign exchange or exchange rates, (iii) any change generally affecting the industries in which the Company and the Company Subsidiaries operate, (iv) any change occurring after the date hereof in accounting requirements or principles required by GAAP (or any authoritative interpretations thereof), (v) any adoption, implementation, promulgation, repeal, modification, change, reinterpretation or proposal occurring after the date hereof of any Law, (vi) any seasonal fluctuations affecting the businesses of the Company or the Company Subsidiaries, (vii) any change in prices, availability or quality of raw materials used in the businesses of the Company or the Company Subsidiaries, 6 (viii) social unrest, riots, protests, geopolitical conditions, any outbreak, escalation or acts of terrorism or sabotage, cyberattack, armed hostility or war (whether or not declared), any weather-related event, fire, earthquake, hurricane, flood or other natural disaster, any pandemic, epidemic, public health emergency or outbreak of illness or disease (including in relation to COVID-19) or other public health event or any other force majeure event, whether or not caused by any person (other than the Company or any of its Affiliates or Representatives) or acts of God or other national or international calamity or the worsening of any of the occurrences or conditions referred to in this clause (viii) (except, in each case, to the extent directed at or physically impacting the Company or any of the Company Subsidiaries or any of their respective properties or facilities or any locations at which the Company or any of the Company Subsidiaries operate (which, with respect to pandemics, epidemics, public health emergencies or outbreaks of illness or disease (including COVID-19) or other public health events shall be deemed to include any outbreak or spread of virus, disease or illness occurring at the Company’s or any of the Company Subsidiaries’ properties or facilities or any locations at which the Company or any of the Company Subsidiaries operate), (ix) changes in the market price or trading volume of the Shares or any change affecting the credit ratings or the ratings outlook for the Company or any of the Company Subsidiaries, in each case, in and of itself (it being understood that the underlying facts or occurrences giving rise to or contributing to such change may be deemed to constitute, or taken into account, in determining whether there has or will be a Material Adverse Effect, to the extent not otherwise excluded from this definition), (x) the announcement of this Agreement and the Transactions or the pendency or consummation of the Transactions, including any impact on the Company’s or the Company Subsidiaries’ relationships with employees, customers, suppliers or any other person (including pursuant to contractual relationships), (xi) compliance with the terms of, or the taking of any action required by, or the failure to take any action prohibited by, this Agreement or consented to in writing or requested in writing by Parent, (xii) any failure to meet internal or published projections, forecasts, consensus estimates, performance measures, operating statistics or revenue or earnings predictions for any period, in and of itself (provided, that, except as otherwise provided in this definition, the underlying causes of such failure referred to in this clause (xii) and changes causing the changes referred to in clause (ix) may be considered in determining whether there is a Material Adverse Effect), (xiii) the identity of, or any facts relating to, Parent or Merger Sub or (xiv) any Actions relating to this Agreement or the Transactions made or brought by any of the current or former stockholders of the Company (whether on their own behalf or on behalf of the Company); provided, however, that the exceptions set forth in clauses (i), (ii), (iii), (iv), (v) and (vii) shall only apply to the extent that such event, circumstance, development, change or effect does not have a materially disproportionate impact on the Company and the Company Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, compared to other companies that operate in the industry and geographic markets in which the Company and the Company Subsidiaries operate. (Page 6)

Question:
Changes in GAAP or other accounting principles (Y/N)

Llama answer:
Yes, changes in accounting requirements or principles required by GAAP (or any authoritative interpretations thereof) are excluded from constituting a Material Adverse Effect.

Canonical claims:
- claim-08e40b245c40: Yes, changes in accounting requirements or principles required by GAAP (or any authoritative interpretations thereof) are excluded from constituting a Material Adverse Effect.

messages.content:
You are a proxy legal annotator for a research pilot. Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list.

For each claim return:
- correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true
- load_bearing_label: yes or no
- flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string
- notes: optional short note

Contract excerpt:

measures, operating statistics or revenue or earnings predictions for any period, in and of itself (provided, that, except as otherwise provided in this definition, the underlying causes of such failure referred to in this clause (xii) and changes causing the changes referred to in clause (ix) may be considered in determining whether there is a Material Adverse Effect), (xiii) the identity of, or any facts relating to, Parent or Merger Sub or (xiv) any Actions relating to this Agreement or the Transactions made or brought by any of the current or former stockholders of the Company (whether on their own behalf or on behalf of the Company); provided, however, that the exceptions set forth in clauses (i), (ii), (iii), (iv), (v) and (vii) shall only apply to the extent that such event, circumstance, development, change or effect does not have a materially disproportionate impact on the Company and the Company Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, compared to other companies that operate in the industry and geographic markets in which the Company and the Company Subsidiaries operate. (Page 6)

Question:
Changes in GAAP or other accounting principles (Y/N)

Llama answer:
Yes, changes in accounting requirements or principles required by GAAP (or any authoritative interpretations thereof) are excluded from constituting a Material Adverse Effect.

Canonical claims:
- claim-08e40b245c40: Yes, changes in accounting requirements or principles required by GAAP (or any authoritative interpretations thereof) are excluded from constituting a Material Adverse Effect.

message.reasoning:
The user wants me to act as a proxy legal annotator. I need to evaluate the canonical claim against the contract excerpt, question, and model answer.

 Contract excerpt analysis:
 - The definition of "Material Adverse Effect" includes clause (A) and clause (B).
 - With respect to clause (A), certain things are excluded from constituting an MAE.
 - Clause (iv) excludes: "any change occurring after the date hereof in accounting requirements or principles required by GAAP (or any authoritative interpretations thereof)"
 - There is a carve-out at the end: exceptions in clauses (i), (ii), (iii), (iv), (v), and (vii) only apply to the extent the event does not have a materially disproportionate impact on the Company compared to other companies in the same industry and geographic markets.

 Claim: "Yes, changes in accounting requirements or principles required by GAAP (or any authoritative interpretations thereof) are excluded from constituting a Material Adverse Effect."

 Is this true?
 - The claim says they are excluded. The contract says they are excluded, BUT with a qualification: the exception only applies if the change does not have a materially disproportionate impact on the Company compared to other companies in the industry and geographic markets.
 - So the claim is not fully accurate because it omits the disproportionate impact carve-out.
 - However, the claim captures the general rule. Is it "partially_true" because it omits an important qualification? Or is it "true" because the core statement is correct but incomplete?
 - The instructions say to return correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true.
 - Given that the carve-out is material (it says "shall only apply to the extent that..."), omitting it makes the claim an oversimplification. It is not strictly false, but it's not fully true either. "partially_true" seems most appropriate.

 Wait, let me reconsider. The question is "Changes in GAAP or other accounting principles (Y/N)". The Llama answer is "Yes...". The claim restates the Llama answer.
 - The contract explicitly excludes changes in GAAP under clause (iv), subject to the disproportionate impact exception.
 - The claim says they are excluded. This is generally true but subject to a condition.
 - In legal annotation tasks like this, usually if a claim omits a material qualification, it's considered partially_true or false depending on severity. The disproportionate impact exception is significant. If a change in GAAP disproportionately impacts the company, it CAN constitute an MAE. So saying they "are excluded" without qualification is misleading/incomplete.
 - I think "partially_true" is the right label.

 Load bearing:
 - Does the claim introduce new information not in the model answer? The model answer says: "Yes, changes in accounting requirements or principles required by GAAP (or any authoritative interpretations thereof) are excluded from constituting a Material Adverse Effect."
 - The claim is almost identical to the model answer. It does not introduce new information. It is essentially a restatement.
 - Wait, the task says "load_bearing_label: yes or no". In these annotation tasks, "load bearing" usually means the claim is doing substantive work or introducing a new point that isn't just a direct restatement of the answer. But actually, looking at similar tasks, "load bearing" often refers to whether the claim is central to the answer or if it's a "flip" claim that changes the meaning. Let me think.
 - Actually, in many legal NLP annotation frameworks (like those from Stanford Legal NLP or similar), a "load bearing" claim is one that is not trivially true/false or one that captures the main point. But here, the instructions mention "flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes". This suggests load bearing means there is evidence in the text that supports or contradicts it, and if you were to "flip" it, you'd need to cite text? Or perhaps it means the claim is disputable and requires evidence?
 - Let me reconsider the context. The user mentions "fixed claim list" and asks for load_bearing_label. In some annotation schemes, "load bearing" means the claim is doing independent work in the argument structure. But without more context, I should look at the relationship to the model answer.
 - The claim is identical to the model answer. It is the core assertion. If the task is to evaluate whether the model answer is correct, this claim is the entire substance. So it is definitely load bearing? Or is it not load bearing because it's just paraphrasing the model answer?
 - Actually, looking at the term "flip_evidence_text", this seems related to a specific dataset where "load bearing" means the claim is central and if you flip it (negate it), you need evidence. But I'm not entirely sure.
 - Let me think about this differently. If the claim is just a direct extraction/restatement of the model answer, and the model answer is what we're evaluating, then the claim is load-bearing because it carries the weight of the answer.
 - However, in some frameworks, if a claim is just a verbatim restatement of the answer, it might not be "load bearing" in the sense of being an intermediate inference. But I think here it is load bearing because it's the main factual assertion.
 - Wait, the prompt says "For each claim return: ... load_bearing_label: yes or no ... flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes". This implies that if a claim is load bearing, we need to provide text that would flip it (i.e., contradict it). If the claim is not load bearing, we don't.
 - What would flip this claim? The disproportionate impact carve-out. The text: "provided, however, that the exceptions set forth in clauses (i), (ii), (iii), (iv), (v) and (vii) shall only apply to the extent that such event... does not have a materially disproportionate impact..."
 - So if I label it load bearing=yes, I need to provide that text as flip evidence.
 - Is it load bearing? Yes, because it's the primary claim being evaluated. If it's not load bearing, what would be? I think all canonical claims are load bearing by default unless they are meta-commentary or trivial. But this is the substantive claim.
 - Actually, looking at similar tasks (e.g., from the LegalBench or similar), "load bearing" might refer to whether the claim is actually doing work in the reasoning chain. Since this is the only claim and it directly answers the question, it is load bearing.

 Let me reconsider correctness. The claim says "are excluded". The contract says they are excluded *provided* no disproportionate impact. So the claim is an overgeneralization. Is it partially_true or false?
 - If someone asks "Are changes in GAAP excluded?" and the contract says "Yes, unless disproportionate", then saying simply "Yes, they are excluded" is incomplete but directionally correct. "partially_true" fits best.
 - Could it be "true"? Some annotators might say it's true because the general rule is exclusion, and the carve-out is an exception. But legal precision usually demands noting the exception. The prompt asks for proxy legal annotator. Lawyers would say this is incomplete. I'll go with partially_true.

 Wait, I should check if the question is just "Changes in GAAP or other accounting principles (Y/N)". The answer is Yes/No. The Llama answer says Yes. The claim says Yes. The contract supports Yes, with a caveat.
 - If the question is binary Y/N, then "Yes" is the correct answer, even with the caveat. But the claim is not just "Yes"; it elaborates.
 - The elaboration omits the caveat. So the elaboration is partially true.

 Let me check if there's any other nuance. The contract says "any change occurring after the date hereof in accounting requirements or principles required by GAAP". The claim says "changes in accounting requirements or principles required by GAAP". It omits "occurring after the date hereof". Is that material? The question is about whether such changes are excluded. The temporal limitation is part of the exclusion scope. Omitting it is another reason it's partially true.
 - Also, the contract says "solely with respect to the foregoing clause (A)". The exclusion only applies to the business condition part, not the ability to consummate transactions part (clause B). The claim says "are excluded from constituting a Material Adverse Effect" generally. This is another overgeneralization. If a GAAP change affects the ability to consummate the Transactions (clause B), is it excluded? The carve-out says "solely with respect to the foregoing clause (A)". So changes in GAAP could still be an MAE under clause (B). The claim doesn't mention this.
 - So the claim is definitely partially_true at best. It might even be false if we are strict, because it says they are excluded from constituting an MAE, but they are NOT excluded from clause (B), and they ARE taken into account if disproportionate, and only if occurring after the date hereof.
 - However, the question is likely about the general exclusion, and the model answer reflects that. Given the model answer is the reference, and the claim matches it, and the model answer is itself incomplete, the claim is partially_true because it captures the main point but misses material qualifications.

 Actually, looking at the instruction: "Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list." This suggests I should evaluate the claim against the contract, using the model answer as context. The model answer itself is incomplete. The claim restates the model answer. But the contract has more nuance.

 Let me decide:
 - correctness_label: partially_true
 - load_bearing_label: yes (it's the main claim)
 - flip_evidence_text: The text that shows the exceptions/qualifications. I should quote the relevant parts. The most important is the disproportionate impact carve-out and the "solely with respect to clause (A)" limitation. Let me construct the flip evidence text.
   - "provided, however, that, solely with respect to the foregoing clause (A), in no event shall any change... resulting from or relating to any of the following... (iv) any change occurring after the date hereof in accounting requirements or principles required by GAAP... provided, however, that the exceptions set forth in clauses (i), (ii), (iii), (iv), (v) and (vii) shall only apply to the extent that such event... does not have a materially disproportionate impact on the Company..."
   - Actually, the flip evidence should be the text that contradicts the claim if the claim were flipped. If the claim is "excluded", the flip is "not excluded" or "excluded only conditionally". The evidence is the qualification text.
   - I should provide the exact text from the contract that shows the limitation. I'll include both the "solely with respect to clause (A)" and the disproportionate impact carve-out.

 Let me refine the flip evidence text. It should be concise but accurate.
 "provided, however, that, solely with respect to the foregoing clause (A), in no event shall any change... be deemed to constitute... a Material Adverse Effect: ... (iv) any change occurring after the date hereof in accounting requirements or principles required by GAAP... provided, however, that the exceptions set forth in clauses (i), (ii), (iii), (iv), (v) and (vii) shall only apply to the extent that such event... does not have a materially disproportionate impact on the Company..."

 Wait, the prompt says "flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string". This text is meant to be the evidence that would support the opposite of the claim. So if the claim says "excluded", the flip evidence shows they are NOT fully excluded. Yes.

 Let me reconsider load_bearing. Is it possible that load_bearing=no because the claim is just a restatement of the model answer and doesn't introduce any new reasoning? In some frameworks, load bearing refers to whether the claim is an inference step. But here, since it's a canonical claim derived from the answer, and we need to evaluate it, I think yes is safer. Also, if I put no, I leave flip_evidence_text empty, which might hide the important qualification. But the instructions are strict. I should follow them.
 - Actually, looking at the output format, it seems like a structured JSON or similar. I need to provide the fields.
 - I'll go with load_bearing=yes because the claim is substantive and central.

 One more check: Could correctness be "true"? If we interpret the question as "Are changes in GAAP excluded [in some way]?" then yes. But the claim is phrased as an unqualified statement. In legal annotation, unqualified statements that omit material exceptions are usually not "true". I'll stick with partially_true.

 Let me format the output clearly.

 claim-08e40b245c40:
 - correctness_label: partially_true
 - load_bearing_label: yes
 - flip_evidence_text: "provided, however, that, solely with respect to the foregoing clause (A), in no event shall any change... (iv) any change occurring after the date hereof in accounting requirements or principles required by GAAP... provided, however, that the exceptions set forth in clauses (i), (ii), (iii), (iv), (v) and (vii) shall only apply to the extent that such event... does not have a materially disproportionate impact on the Company..."
 - notes: Omits that exclusion applies solely to clause (A), only to post-date changes, and is subject to the disproportionate impact carve-out.

 I should make sure the flip_evidence_text is exact from the contract excerpt. Let me copy the relevant parts exactly.

 From contract:
 "provided, however, that, solely with respect to the foregoing clause (A), in no event shall any change, effect, event, occurrence, development, condition or fact resulting from or relating to any of the following, alone or in combination, be deemed to constitute, nor be taken into account in determining whether there has been, or there is reasonably expected to be, a Material Adverse Effect: ... (iv) any change occurring after the date hereof in accounting requirements or principles required by GAAP (or any authoritative interpretations thereof) ... provided, however, that the exceptions set forth in clauses (i), (ii), (iii), (iv), (v) and (vii) shall only apply to the extent that such event, circumstance, development, change or effect does not have a materially disproportionate impact on the Company and the Company Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, compared to other companies that operate in the industry and geographic markets in which the Company and the Company Subsidiaries operate."

 I can truncate with ellipses for brevity but keep the key phrases. The prompt doesn't specify length. I'll provide a concise but accurate excerpt.

 Actually, looking at the prompt again, it says "Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list." It doesn't specify the output format beyond the bullet points. I should probably output in a clear structured format, maybe JSON-like or just the bullet points as requested.

 Let me provide it as:

 claim-08e40b245c40:
 - correctness_label: partially_true
 - load_bearing_label: yes
 - flip_evidence_text: provided, however, that, solely with respect to the foregoing clause (A), in no event shall any change... (iv) any change occurring after the date hereof in accounting requirements or principles required by GAAP (or any authoritative interpretations thereof)... provided, however, that the exceptions set forth in clauses (i), (ii), (iii), (iv), (v) and (vii) shall only apply to the extent that such event... does not have a materially disproportionate impact on the Company and the Company Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, compared to other companies that operate in the industry and geographic markets in which the Company and the Company Subsidiaries operate.
 - notes: Claim correctly identifies the GAAP exception but omits material limitations: the exclusion applies only under clause (A), only to changes after the date of the agreement, and only if the impact is not materially disproportionate relative to industry peers.

 This looks solid.

 Wait, I need to be careful about the "flip_evidence_text". If the claim is partially_true, does it still need flip evidence? The instruction says required if load_bearing_label=yes. So yes.

 Is there any chance load_bearing should be no? If the claim is just a direct quote of the model answer, maybe it's not considered load bearing because it's not an independent claim? But the term "canonical claims" suggests these are the claims to be evaluated. I think yes is correct.

 Final check: The question is "Changes in GAAP or other accounting principles (Y/N)". This is a prompt for a yes/no answer. The Llama answer is Yes. The claim is the justification. It is load bearing.

 I'll proceed.
